events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: This is a Testora Studio I E L T S Listening Practice Test. The test will begin in five seconds.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section 1. You will hear a telephone conversation between a man who wants to hire a hall and a community centre officer. First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 10.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Good morning, Riverside Community Centre, Diane speaking. How can I help you?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Hello. I'd like to hire one of your halls for a party, if any are free next month.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Of course. Let me take a few details and start a booking form. Can I have your surname please?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Yes, it's Hartley.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Could you spell that for me?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Certainly. H, A, R, T, L, E, Y.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Hartley, thank you. And what kind of event is it?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: It's a retirement party for my father. He's finishing work after forty years.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: How lovely. What date were you thinking of?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: The sixth of May, a Saturday.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Let me check. I'm afraid the sixth is already booked for a wedding.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Oh. What about the following Saturday then?

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: The sixteenth of May? Yes, that's completely free.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Great, let's say the sixteenth of May.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: We have two rooms available that day. The Oak Room holds forty people, and the larger Garden Room holds up to a hundred.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: We're expecting about seventy guests, so the Oak Room would be too small.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: In that case I'll put you down for the Garden Room. And roughly how many guests did you say?

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Seventy. Maybe a few more, but seventy to be safe.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: That's fine. Now, the cost. On weekdays the hall is thirty pounds an hour, but on Saturdays it's forty pounds an hour.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Forty an hour on the Saturday, understood.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: There's also a refundable deposit of sixty pounds, which you get back if there's no damage.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: No problem. Do you provide any equipment?

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: We have a projector available, but most people hiring the Garden Room ask for a microphone, as it's quite a big space.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Yes, we'll definitely need a microphone for the speeches. We won't need the projector.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: I'll note down a microphone. One more thing about access: parking is not at the front, it's at the rear of the building.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: At the rear, got it.

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: And finally, a mobile number so I can confirm the booking?

events.speaker: Officer
events.text: Let me read that back: 0 7 8 1 1, 9 0 4 2 1 7. Perfect. I'll send a confirmation shortly.

events.speaker: Caller
events.text: Thank you very much. Goodbye.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section 2. You will hear a guide giving an introductory talk to a group of visitors at Greenfield Country Park. First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 20.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: Good morning everyone, and welcome to Greenfield Country Park. My name is Sanjay and I'll give you a quick introduction before you explore.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: A little history first. People often assume this land was once farmland, but in fact, for most of the nineteenth century it was a working stone quarry. The lake you can see today was the main pit, which slowly filled with water after the quarry closed.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: Let's talk about opening times. The park itself is open from dawn until dusk all year round. The visitor centre, however, has shorter hours. In winter it closes at four, but in summer, which is now, it stays open until six in the evening, so you have plenty of time.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: A word about dogs, because many of you have brought them. Dogs are welcome across most of the park and can run freely in the woodland. However, near the lake they must be kept on leads at all times, because of the nesting birds.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: Now, people enjoy lots of activities here. We have walking trails, fishing, and cycling. But by far the most popular activity, especially with families, is bird watching. The lake attracts dozens of rare species and our hides are busy every weekend.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: And some news. We're always improving the park. Last year we resurfaced the car park, and next month we're very excited to be opening a brand new café overlooking the water. So do come back and visit it.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: Right, let me tell you where to find things, because the park is large. If you'd like to hire a bicycle, the hire point is down at the Lakeside, next to the boats.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: The picnic area is the opposite direction, tucked away in the Woodland, where there's plenty of shade on a hot day like today.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: For the best views, climb up to the Hilltop, where you'll find our observation tower. On a clear day you can see for miles.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: If you need the toilets, the nearest ones are right here at the North Gate, where you came in.

events.speaker: Guide
events.text: And finally, for souvenirs and postcards, the gift shop is housed in the beautifully restored Old Mill, just across the bridge. Enjoy your visit!

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section 3. You will hear two students, Tom and Lena, discussing their geography field-trip report with their tutor. First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 30.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Come in, Tom, Lena. So, how did the river field trip go? I've read your draft report.

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: It went well overall, but honestly the hardest part wasn't the writing. It was collecting the data, because it rained heavily on both days and our equipment kept slipping.

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: Yes, the weather really slowed us down. The analysis afterwards was actually fine.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: I see. Well, the report reads clearly, but my main suggestion is that you add more graphs. At the moment you describe the numbers in words, and a couple of charts would make the trends much easier to follow.

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: That's fair. We can turn the tables into bar charts.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Good. Now, your central finding. What would you say your main conclusion is?

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: That the water quality actually improved as we moved downstream, which surprised us, because we expected it to get worse near the town.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: That's a strong conclusion, and worth highlighting. For the next stage, what do you think you need to do?

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: I thought we should collect more samples.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: You have enough samples, actually. What's missing is the human side. I'd like you to interview some local residents about how they use the river. That would add real depth.

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: Okay, interviewing residents, that makes sense.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: And remember the deadline. The full report is due at the end of the month, so don't leave the interviews too late.

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: End of the month, noted. Should we divide the remaining work?

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Good idea. Let's agree who does what. Who'll write the introduction?

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: I'll do the introduction. I've already started it.

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: And I'm happy to draw the maps, since I did the GPS work.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Perfect. Now, someone needs to contact the council for the historical pollution figures.

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: We could both do that. Lena knows the office and I've got the reference numbers.

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: Yes, let's contact the council together.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Fine. And the reference list, that's fiddly. Tom, can you check the references?

events.speaker: Tom
events.text: Sure, I'll check all the references.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Which leaves the presentation slides. Lena?

events.speaker: Lena
events.text: Yes, I'll prepare the presentation. I enjoy that part.

events.speaker: Tutor
events.text: Excellent. That's a clear plan. See me again next week.

events.speaker: Narrator
events.text: Section 4. You will hear part of a university lecture about green roofs, that is, roofs covered with plants. First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at green roofs, roofs that are deliberately covered with growing plants, and why cities are increasingly adopting them.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Although the idea is ancient, the modern green roof movement really began in Germany in the nineteen sixties and seventies. German engineers developed most of the early standards, and the country still has more green roofs than anywhere else.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: So why build them? The first major benefit is temperature. Cities are much hotter than the countryside, a problem we call the urban heat island. Green roofs absorb less heat than concrete, and so they help to reduce this island effect across a city.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Let's look at how one is built, from the bottom up. Directly on the roof deck goes a waterproof layer. Above that, crucially, sits a special membrane, whose job is to stop plant roots from damaging the waterproofing below. Without it, roots would eventually cause leaks.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: On top of that go a drainage layer, a thin growing medium, and finally the plants. Now, the plants are chosen very carefully. Because the soil is shallow and dries quickly, designers favour succulents, plants that store water in their leaves and so survive long dry periods with almost no maintenance.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Beyond cooling, green roofs bring other benefits. When it rains, an ordinary roof sends water straight into the drains all at once. A green roof soaks up much of the rain, which significantly reduces runoff and helps prevent urban flooding.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: There's an ecological benefit too. In dense cities with few gardens, these roofs create surprising islands of life. They provide a habitat for insects, particularly bees and butterflies, which struggle to find flowers at street level.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Of course, there are drawbacks. The most obvious one is money. While they save on energy bills over time, the main disadvantage of a green roof is the high installation cost, which can deter building owners.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Maintenance matters as well. Although low, it isn't zero. In particular, the roof must be inspected regularly for leaks, because water damage can be expensive and hard to trace once it spreads.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Let me summarise the climate benefits. In summer, the layer of soil and plants insulates the building and keeps the rooms below noticeably cool, cutting air-conditioning use. In winter, the same layer helps keep heat in.

events.speaker: Lecturer
events.text: Looking ahead, I think the trend is clear. Several cities already offer grants, and I predict that within a decade green roofs will become a legal requirement for many new buildings, just as insulation is today. We'll stop there for now.
